does: This paper proposes a primal-dual direct preference optimization (DPO) approach for constrained LLM alignment that maximizes reward while keeping costs below a specified threshold. The method operates in two stages: first training a model using standard DPO on reward preference data, then fine-tuning using a rearranged Lagrangian DPO objective that leverages the learned reward information. The approach requires training only two models rather than three, significantly reducing memory overhead, and crucially establishes rigorous theoretical guarantees on suboptimality and constraint violation. The method avoids requiring prior knowledge of optimal solutions, addressing a key limitation of prior constrained optimization approaches for LLMs.
connects: This work builds on the direct preference optimization (DPO) framework, which has become central to modern LLM alignment by optimizing preferences directly without requiring explicit reward model training. It extends DPO to the constrained optimization setting, connecting to broader literature on constrained reinforcement learning and constrained optimization in machine learning. The paper responds to limitations in prior safety alignment work that either incurs prohibitive computational costs through multi-model training or requires impractical prior knowledge about optimal solutions. It also engages with Lagrangian methods from convex optimization, adapting classical primal-dual techniques to the non-convex LLM fine-tuning setting while providing convergence guarantees.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of constrained alignment techniques for LLMs with formal safety guarantees and lower computational barriers. The two-model approach makes constraint-respecting alignment accessible to practitioners with limited resources compared to prior three-model methods. The established convergence guarantees create a foundation for future theoretical analysis of constrained LLM optimization and hybrid approaches combining preference learning with explicit constraints. This could facilitate development of safer LLM systems that simultaneously optimize for helpfulness while adhering to regulatory or safety constraints, opening applications in high-stakes domains where both reward maximization and constraint satisfaction are critical.
